notes: THE_WORKSHOP — Frasier's technical board. Tech threads. Hidden technical unlocks. Sprint 2 fills it out to 5 threads.

threads.subject: STICKY · the toolbox · what's in it, what's not

op.body:
Pinned since the workshop was a workshop.

What's in the cathedral office toolbox at any given time:
  · the Klein lineman's pliers (Pop's, '64)
  · a Wiha #2 phillips, a Wiha #1, and two flatheads
  · a 25-foot Stanley tape (replaced '94 after the boathouse fire)
  · the Fluke 87 multimeter (mine, '91)
  · a soldering iron that runs hot, with the original Weller tip
  · the spare modem and two phone jacks
  · spare V.32 cables in the lower drawer
  · everything else is in JF's truck or at the storefront

What's NOT in the toolbox:
  · the heavy gear
  · the binding work

The binding work is the matriarch's. The heavy gear is the rest stop's. Don't ask me on this board.

— F.

threads.subject: the 14400 USR sportster · still on the original ROM

op.body:
The modem on RUST_CODE is still the USR Sportster I put in in October '92. ROM has never been flashed. The carrier negotiation is the carrier negotiation that worked in '92 and it still works.

There is a newer Sportster in JF's truck, never opened. It's an emergency spare. The day I open the box is the day the original gives up. The original has not yet given up.

WIRE_MOTHER says the original will go in August. The original disagrees.

— F.

replies.body: Foal — the original goes in August. The matriarch has been right about modems since 1986.

threads.subject: where do you put a heavy thing at the end of the night

op.body:
When the heavy gear gets used hard during the week, it needs a rest stop at the end. I've been running a quiet line for that since '92. Dial 5550108. The rest is the rest. If you've been working the heavy gear and you don't know the dial, you should.

  — F.

replies.body: Confirmed. The rest is the rest.

threads.subject: the cathedral AC · what failed and what to swap

op.body:
Window unit gave up on Wednesday at 11 AM. Compressor's seized. Replacement is a window-mounted 18000 BTU; the bracket's the same; the wiring's old enough that the new unit's pigtail is gonna want an adapter. If anyone's done this swap on a cathedral installation, the wiring run was the surprise — what did you do for the conduit?

  — F.

replies.body: We did the same swap on the boathouse in '93. The conduit was the surprise then too. The trick is fishing a new run alongside the old without pulling the old; the old's the structural ground for the original.
Don't run AC off the same panel as the lighting. We learned that one in '88.

threads.subject: the modem on RUST_CODE · slow nights and the carrier

op.body:
Carrier's dropping on Sundays after 10 PM. The line into the cathedral basement runs alongside the boiler-room conduit and the boiler kicks on for the rectory hot-water cycle at 10:08. I've been there. The fix is moving the modem line to the office run; I'll do it Saturday. Heads up if you can't get on Sunday between 10 and 11 — that's why.

  — F.

replies.body: Confirmed. We had the same problem in '93 on the line that ran through the wellhouse. The fix is what you said. Saturday's fine; the rest of the circle doesn't post Sunday night anyway.

threads.subject: the gauge in the boiler room · what number means what

op.body:
The Bourdon-tube gauge on the cathedral boiler is reading 12 psi cold, which means the dial is off by 2 psi or the system is overpressurized by 2 psi. Pop tagged it with a piece of red tape in '91 — the tape is still there. I'm going to assume the dial is off because Pop knew the gauge and the tape is the calibration mark.

If anyone has a fresh gauge they don't need, the cathedral wants it.

replies.body: Is the red tape at 12 cold or at the operating pressure mark? They're different calibrations.
Cold. Pop's tape was for the resting pressure not the operating mark. Confirmed by his note in the binder.

threads.subject: what's a fair price for the '91 Bell trunk cards

op.body:
Bell's auctioning off the '91 trunk cards from the south parish hub upgrade. The cards aren't useful for anything current — but they're the same generation as the boards in the cathedral's PBX, so spares are spares. Auction's the 18th. What's a fair price per card? My ceiling's $40. Above that I walk.

  — F.

replies.body: Did the cards come from the south parish hub or the regional uplink? Different generations.
$25-30 is fair. $40 is the price for someone in a hurry. Don't be that someone.

threads.subject: WGUR's carrier-overload event · what actually happens at the transmitter

op.body:
For anyone who read the Gambit piece on Evangeline Babineaux's after-event report at WGUR: the technical story matters here even if the human story is what you came for.

The carrier at WGUR is a 900W xenon feeding through a Continental 314R-1 transmitter. When the render queue at Ev's terminal wouldn't drain, the transmitter didn't fail — it kept modulating a signal that had no coherent packet-form to modulate. The 'off signal that wouldn't turn off' is a specific failure mode: the transmitter is CORRECTLY doing what it's asked to do; the queue is asking it to modulate a signal whose destination handshake has been lost.

The kill switch cuts the transmitter's B+ supply. Doesn't cut the queue. To cut the queue you have to sever the trunk at the patch panel — which is what Ev did at 3:51 AM. This is why the after-event report says 'trunk severed at patch panel' and not 'transmitter shut down.'

If you're running any transmitter in the parish · Weir, JF, Rashid at WGUR · know the difference. The transmitter is the last thing to blame.

  — F.

replies.body: F · yes. The trunk-sever is the correct move. Ev knew it in three seconds because she has been in that shack every Thursday for eight years. Do not be the person who runs a transmitter you have only been at three months. — S.

The 'kill switch' being not-the-actual-kill is a whole essay in the shape of what tools ACTUALLY do vs what they LOOK LIKE they should do. My grandfather in Slaton had the same problem with the tractor's emergency shutoff in 1964. It didn't. He learned. The tractor stayed. — h.

threads.subject: the cathedral basement PBX · W14 relay checklist

op.body:
STICKY through Aug 27. STEEPLE's OT_005 designated the cathedral basement as the primary inland relay for the W14 storm window. Here's the technical checklist for the JF-at-the-boiler + JF-at-the-desk shift:

  1. Boiler: Continental Model 46 · winter cycle active from Aug 20 · pilot check twice daily · relief valve open to 15 PSI
  2. Desk: CB radio modified for the relay frequency (147.180 MHz simplex) · powered from the boiler-room 12V bus · antenna is the cathedral spire copper
  3. Cross-check: STEEPLE calls at 9 PM / 11 PM / 1 AM / 3 AM / 5 AM · JF acks · notebook logs the carrier read each time
  4. Fallback: if the boiler cuts out, the 12V bus drops · the radio drops · Venice depot at 5551849 is the fallback
  5. Civilian: MS_010 volunteer at the desk is Mrs Trujillo (12 AM - 4 AM). She has been briefed. She has the notebook. She is not to touch the boiler.

Any questions before Sunday · reply here.

  — F.

replies.body: Boiler cycle set. Radio bench-tested Thursday. Notebook on the desk. Mrs T comes in at 11:45. All-clear my end.
Is the antenna the cathedral spire copper or the cathedral spire copper plus the roof grounding grid?
TQ · both. The grounding grid is part of the antenna in this configuration. Not documented on the FCC filing. It's why the reception is what it is. — F.

threads.subject: the wgur zeiss lens · STEEPLE bought it · replacement schedule for Fresnel #47-C

op.body:
Bought the Zeiss lens from Ollie at the Static Drive-In yesterday afternoon. $200 as agreed. She said she liked it going to someone who would use it.

The lens goes on the maintenance schedule for buoy #47-C at Pass-A-Loutre. The '84 Fresnel currently on 47-C is scheduled for replacement in Q1 '97. The Zeiss is a drop-in with a housing swap I've already fabbed at the Venice depot machine shop (60-minute job).

Delphine at Christian Ice was at the plant when I picked up the lens — she saw the transaction. She asked me why the Coast Guard was buying a movie lens. I told her the movie lens is a lighthouse lens if the lighthouse is at a movie's distance. She laughed. She said Emile would have liked that.

  — S.

replies.body: The lens goes where it's useful. Emile Christian and Ollie Blanchard both spent their careers making light useful. The lens is the through-line. — F.
